romanized: Fo Shou Gua (qu pi shui zhu)

region_of_origin: Central America (widely cultivated across Southeast Asia and Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a chayote entry (FOODON_03301710); the boiled and peeled preparation is not separately coded — closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LT covers chayote as a commodity; boiled/peeled preparation facet not separately enumerated — closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chayote or its preparations in publicly available SNOMED CT browser as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled peeled chayote is approximately 100 g, equivalent to roughly half a medium chayote, served as a side vegetable dish.

nutrient_source.title: Chayote, fruit, raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for boiled peeled chayote (F14040026). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for chayote, fruit, raw (FDC ID 168409) where HPB does not report fatty acid fractions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for boiled peeled chayote was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 28 is estimated from the closest analogous low-GI non-starchy vegetable (summer squash / zucchini, boiled) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (28 × 5.09) / 100 ≈ 1.4 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100 g) and high potassium (173 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled peeled chayote is an excellent choice for hypertension management: its sodium content of 1 mg/100 g is negligible and its potassium-to-sodium ratio supports arterial vasodilation consistent with DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~28), low carbohydrate (5.09 g/100 g), and moderate dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of approximately 28 and only 5.09 g carbohydrate per 100 g, boiled peeled chayote produces a glycemic load of approximately 1.4 per serving, making it a safe and filling vegetable for blood glucose control in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.11 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and 2.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled peeled chayote contributes negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol, while its dietary fibre content of 2.8 g/100 g supports LDL cholesterol reduction, making it a heart-healthy vegetable suitable for cardiovascular disease prevention diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C (~7.7 mg/100 g) as antioxidant; negligible saturated fat and zero trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled peeled chayote provides modest vitamin C as an antioxidant and carries no pro-inflammatory fat burden; however, it lacks omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or other established anti-inflammatory factors, so it is a neutral rather than actively therapeutic choice for rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled peeled chayote (Sechium edule) is a minimally processed non-leafy vegetable prepared by removing the thin pale-green skin and boiling the firm white flesh until tender. The cooked vegetable has a mild, slightly sweet flavour with a soft, slightly crisp texture reminiscent of cooked zucchini or cucumber. Chayote is widely consumed across Southeast Asia, Latin America, and the Caribbean, and is a common side dish in Singaporean and Malaysian home cooking, often served plain, stir-fried, or in soups. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides only 24 kcal, 0.62 g protein, 0.48 g fat, and 5.09 g carbohydrate, with 2.8 g dietary fibre and a very low sodium content of 1 mg. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 28 places it firmly in the low-GI category, making it suitable for blood glucose management. The potassium content of 173 mg/100 g supports cardiovascular and blood pressure health. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is appropriate for weight management, diabetes, hypertension, and heart disease prevention diets. The food is also known as choko (Australia/New Zealand), mirliton (Louisiana/Caribbean), and vegetable pear.
common_user_queries: How many calories in boiled chayote?
What is the glycemic index of chayote?
Is chayote good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (canonical primary source for Singapore foods, confidence 0.95 per input record). Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions estimated from USDA FoodData Central for raw chayote (FDC ID 168409) with minor adaptation for boiled preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed international GI table; this is clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.11 g), monounsaturated fat (0.03 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.19 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168409 (chayote, fruit, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry; values are plausible for a low-fat vegetable. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g — confirmed negligible for a plain boiled vegetable with no added fats. 3. Vitamin C (7.7 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central for raw chayote; boiling may reduce this by 20–30%. 4. Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved sources): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for boiled peeled chayote are not fully reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. 5. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on summer squash/zucchini (boiled) from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for boiled chayote was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — confirmed zero for a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. 7. Rheumatoid arthritis suitability from input record has been retained in additional_conditions with corrected reasoning: chayote is nutritionally neutral for RA (no established anti-inflammatory factors such as omega-3, polyphenols, or >3 g whole-food fibre per serving), and the suitable flag has been set to low concern rather than false/unsuitable.
